review_text: Every few years since 2005, I've watched Schindler's List on the 27th January, National Holocaust Memorial Day here in the UK. Sometimes literature and movies are the only way to educate, to remember, and to honour those who lost their lives many years ago. Spielberg's film is a testament to the humanitarianism of one righteous man who took a stand and saved 1200 Jews from almost certain death, but also about the human spirit that dwells within us all. A landmark film that has a timeless quality to it courtesy of that incredible cinematography from Janusz Kaminski, it's Spielberg's finest work behind a camera.

review_text: Conflicting for a lot of reasons. Clearly something with a lot of genuine craft behind it, incredible acting performances, and moving screenwriting. However, the question of the representability of the Holocaust on screen as a cinematic venture, not merely as just documentation, looms over this picture. The use of actual events of the mass murder of an entire race as a means of suspense, to keep the audience on the edge of their seats, it's something that can't and doesn't sit well with me, regardless of the intentions Spielberg may have had in producing the film.

His decision to not storyboard beforehand pays off - it feels more honest than anything else he's ever made.Janusz Kamiński should work in black & white more often. review_text: Schindler’s List is a haunting masterpiece that doesn’t just tell history but also makes us feel it. Spielberg’s stark black-and-white cinematography strips life down to its rawest elements, while the haunting score and unforgettable performances bring the human cost of the Holocaust to the forefront. The girl in the red coat pierces through the horror, a single symbol of innocence lost that shakes both Schindler and the audience. And the shift to color at the end reminds us that life, hope, and survival persisted because of one man’s courage.It’s devastating, unforgettable, and a film that lingers long after the credits roll.

This alone demonstrates how seriously the film approaches the subject of the Holocaust.From World War II to the present day, countless wars and conflicts have erupted. However, innocent civilians, especially children, must never have their lives threatened because of race or religion (this is not to equate all events).This is truly an essential film.

review_text: I am always moved by this film. The task of representing the Holocaust on screen is a really weighty responsibility, and Spielberg deserves a lot of credit for pulling it off this well. He found a way to take one of the singular worst things to happen in human history, and tell an uplifting story about a man searching for redemption, while still being brutally truthful about the sheer scale of human suffering. It is a profound work, and one of my favorite Spielberg movies, but kind of an exhausting experience to watch it.

review_text: I always feel nervous when viewing art made about the Holocaust or other historical atrocities because I believe that good art expresses truth and beauty. If the art depicts the truth of pure evil, then the art will not be beautiful. Even worse, if the art attempts to derive beauty from pure evil, then the art will not be truthful. But for a three hour film entirely about the Holocaust, there are surprisingly few creative decisions that made me queasy, and the net good of this monument to the six million lives lost is unmistakable.

review_text: O realismo é tão forte que parece até um documentário, é cru, é humano, é sombrio. Mostra os horrores do holocausto, toda a dor causada e ainda traz contrapontos com os personagens nazistas, trazendo humanidade a eles, assim mostrando que não foram fantasmas que fizeram tudo aquilo, foram pessoas, humanos chegaram aquele ponto e isso faz com que a história fique mais difícil de engolir. O final é antológico, a fotografia que desde o início é em preto e branco ganha cor, simbolizando a liberdade. É um filme muito poderoso! Neste trabalho Steven Spielberg alcançou seu ápice, é sua direção mais corajosa.

While E.T. remains my personal favorite Spielberg film, this is easily the best film in his filmography.
